questions.q: In the /etc/passwd line 'root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash', what are the seven fields in order?
options: login : password marker : UID : primary GID : comment : home directory : shell
login : UID : GID : password : shell : home : comment
login : password : home : shell : UID : GID : comment
UID : login : GID : comment : home : shell : password
questions.why: The seven /etc/passwd fields are: login name, password marker (x), UID, primary GID, comment, home directory, and shell.

questions.q: In /etc/passwd, what does an 'x' in the second (password) field mean?
options: The user has a password, whose encrypted form is stored in /etc/shadow
The user has no password
The account is locked
The password is 'x'
questions.why: x marks that a password exists; the actual encrypted password is in the second field of /etc/shadow.

questions.q: In /etc/shadow, what does the second field hold, and which algorithm does Rocky use by default?
options: The encrypted password, using SHA512 (set by ENCRYPT_METHOD in /etc/login.defs)
The plain-text password
The UID
The account expiry date
questions.why: Field 2 is the encrypted password; the default algorithm is SHA512, defined by ENCRYPT_METHOD in /etc/login.defs.

questions.q: How is the 'last password change' time (field 3 of /etc/shadow) expressed?
options: As a number of days since 1 January 1970 (a day-based timestamp)
As a full date and time string
As seconds since boot
As a Unix epoch in seconds
questions.why: Shadow ageing fields count DAYS since 1970-01-01; +1 for each day that passes.

questions.q: What is the difference between password expiration and account expiration in /etc/shadow?
options: Password expiry stops login by password; account expiry stops the user logging in at all
They are the same thing
Password expiry deletes the account; account expiry only warns
Account expiry only affects root
questions.why: A password expiry blocks login via the password (field 5); an account expiry (field 8) bars the user from logging in at all.

questions.q: What relationship must hold between /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow?
options: Every line in /etc/passwd must have a corresponding line in /etc/shadow
They are independent files
shadow may contain users that passwd does not
Only privileged users appear in shadow
questions.why: For each /etc/passwd line there must be a matching /etc/shadow line.
